title: Pedestrian Accidents
description: Pedestrians struck by vehicles face some of the most severe injuries of any accident type. California and Arizona laws provide important protections for pedestrians, but prompt action, strong documentation, and medical evaluation are critical to protecting your health and your options.
metaTitle: Pedestrian Accident Guide — What To Do After Being Hit by a Car
metaDescription: What to do after a pedestrian accident in California or Arizona. Immediate steps, evidence checklist, legal rights, and when to speak with an attorney.

commonCauses.title: Failure to Yield at Crosswalks
commonCauses.description: Drivers are required to yield to pedestrians in marked and unmarked crosswalks. Failure to do so is a leading cause of pedestrian accidents.

commonCauses.title: Distracted Driving
commonCauses.description: Drivers using phones or otherwise distracted are less likely to see pedestrians, particularly at intersections and in parking areas.

commonCauses.title: Running Red Lights and Stop Signs
commonCauses.description: Traffic violations that allow a vehicle to enter a pedestrian's right-of-way unexpectedly and at speed.

commonCauses.title: Speeding
commonCauses.description: Higher vehicle speeds dramatically increase the severity of pedestrian injuries. A pedestrian struck at 40 mph has a much lower survival rate than one struck at 20 mph.

commonCauses.title: Left and Right Turns
commonCauses.description: Drivers making turns often focus on traffic rather than pedestrians in the crosswalk. Turning vehicles are responsible for a large percentage of pedestrian crashes.

commonCauses.title: Backing Vehicles
commonCauses.description: Backing cars in driveways, parking lots, and loading areas frequently fail to detect pedestrians, especially children.

commonCauses.title: Poor Lighting or Visibility
commonCauses.description: Night-time, poor weather, and inadequate street lighting reduce pedestrian visibility to drivers.

immediateSteps.title: Call 911
immediateSteps.description: Pedestrian accidents frequently involve serious injuries. Call for emergency services immediately.

immediateSteps.title: Do not get up if you feel neck or back pain
immediateSteps.description: Spinal injuries are common in pedestrian accidents. Stay still until emergency responders arrive unless you are in immediate danger from traffic.

immediateSteps.title: Ask someone to document the scene
immediateSteps.description: If you are unable to take photos, ask a bystander to photograph vehicle positions, the crosswalk, traffic signals, and road conditions.

immediateSteps.title: Get the driver's information
immediateSteps.description: Name, license number, insurance company, and contact information. Do not discuss fault.

immediateSteps.title: Get witness information
immediateSteps.description: Names and contact details from bystanders. Multiple witnesses strengthen a pedestrian's account significantly.

immediateSteps.title: Note the traffic signal status and crosswalk type
immediateSteps.description: Was the light green? Was there a walk signal? Was it a marked or unmarked crosswalk? These details matter for establishing right-of-way.

immediateSteps.title: Seek emergency medical evaluation
immediateSteps.description: Accept ambulance transport if offered. Internal injuries and brain injuries from pedestrian impacts are often not immediately apparent.

immediateSteps.title: Keep all clothing and personal items
immediateSteps.description: Torn clothing, damaged shoes, and broken glasses are physical evidence. Preserve them rather than discarding.

items: Photos of the vehicle that struck you and all damage
Photos of the crosswalk, intersection, and traffic signals
Driver's license, insurance, and contact information
Names and contact info of all witnesses
Torn or damaged clothing (preserve, do not wash)
Photos of injuries taken at the scene and over the following days
Emergency room records and ambulance transport records
Traffic camera or surveillance video (request quickly — footage is often overwritten)
Photos of the accident location taken afterward
Documentation of crosswalk condition, visibility, or signage defects

timelineRisks.action: Traffic cameras and nearby business security footage typically overwrite within 24-72 hours. An attorney can send a preservation request quickly.
Get a full medical evaluation even if you feel functional. Internal injuries and traumatic brain injuries can emerge over days.
Notify your own insurer. If the at-fault driver is uninsured, your own uninsured motorist coverage may apply.
Collect written witness statements while memories are fresh.
If a government vehicle (bus, city car) struck you, California and Arizona have shorter claim notice deadlines — as short as 6 months. This is a hard deadline.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for personal injury
timelineRisks.action: Both states have a 2-year personal injury statute of limitations from the date of injury for non-government defendants.

insuranceIssues.explanation: Pedestrians are particularly vulnerable to being struck by uninsured drivers. Your own auto insurance UM/UIM coverage can apply even if you were not in a vehicle.

Insurance adjusters may argue the pedestrian was jaywalking, not in the crosswalk, or not paying attention. California and Arizona both use pure comparative fault, so even partial fault does not bar recovery.
If struck by a city bus, police vehicle, or other government vehicle, special claims procedures and shorter deadlines apply. These must be handled differently than private party claims.
Pedestrian injuries are often severe and long-term. Accepting a quick settlement before the full extent of injuries is known may leave significant compensation on the table.

whenToGetLawyer: You sustained injuries requiring medical treatment
The driver was uninsured or fled the scene
Fault is being disputed or shared blame is suggested
A government vehicle (bus, city car) was involved
Your injuries involved a hospital stay or long recovery
You received a quick settlement offer before understanding your full injuries
Surveillance footage needs to be preserved before it is overwritten
